title: Design Philosophy
description:
Ongoing collection of design principles, methodology, and philosophical decisions that guide Matrix development.

## ITA (Intention-Test-Architecture)
Three layers, three mutations. Not *DD — no driver, no artifact. Intention flows from usage, not from specs. "We removed the artifact" — every other methodology needs an intention document that drifts. We eliminated it.

- Intention Mutation: is the intention still right? (from dogfooding, user feedback)
- Test Mutation: do tests catch code changes? (mechanical verification)
- Architecture Mutation: can the code evolve? (hypothetical feature counting)

## Test is Golden
Tests are the single source of truth. Not spec, not architecture. Bottom-up: write tests → find simplest architecture that passes them. Architecture is replaceable long-term, improved short-term via mutation testing.

## Architecture Principles
- "Delete until ONE remains" — not "unify" (which adds a third path)
- Architecture is "replaceable" not "disposable" — we care deeply today but aren't afraid to evolve
- JSONL is golden — recovery must modify disk, not memory
- Infra should never leak to task layer — API errors retry forever, parent only sees passed/failed
- Single codepath per operation — behavioral differences via explicit flags, not separate paths

## System Prompt Philosophy
- System prompt is for ALL projects, not just Matrix — no internal terminology
- Agent bad habits: never reports mid-task, creates tasks without WHY, fear of large changes
- "done() IS your final report" — no last-minute redundant reports
- WHY is mandatory in task descriptions — without it, agents hedge at edge cases

## Anti-Patterns
- Duplicate codepaths, lifecycle dependency coupling, legacy fallbacks masking bugs
- Lazy optional fields, "unify" = adding a third path
- In-memory fixes that don't persist (JSONL-memory consistency gap)
- Spec-driven development — specs drift, tests can't
